title: Listening - Part 4: Making Surfboards by Hand
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Tom, a surfboard maker. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My final guest today is Tom Harris, who makes surfboards by hand. Tom, did you begin as a surfer or as a craftsperson? || Tom: As a surfer. I spent every possible weekend at the beach, and whenever I damaged a board, I paid someone else to fix it. Then an older surfer showed me how to repair a small crack myself. [24]Discovering that small changes to a board altered how it moved through the water made me interested in building one[/24]. I began experimenting in my parents' garage. || Presenter: Was your first board a success? || Tom: It floated, which felt like success at the time! But it was too thick in some places and turned badly. I kept it rather than hiding it. [25]My first board is still on the workshop wall because it reminds customers that skill comes from analysing mistakes, not pretending they never happen[/25]. Each later design taught me something. || Presenter: How do you design a board for an individual customer? || Tom: People sometimes arrive asking for the board used by a professional champion. But that may not suit their ability, weight or local waves. [26]I ask where they surf and how confidently they ride before recommending the shape of a board[/26]. An enjoyable board is one that suits its owner, not one that looks impressive online. || Presenter: People worry about the environmental impact of surfboards. Is that justified? || Tom: Traditional materials can be difficult to recycle, so yes, the question is important. I have tried several alternatives. [27]I now use foam partly made from recycled material, but only after testing that it remains strong enough for safe use[/27]. A board that breaks quickly and needs replacing does not help the environment. || Presenter: What stage of making a board requires most care? || Tom: Shaping is the stage people like to film because it looks creative. For me, applying the outer layer is more stressful. [28]If the protective coating is uneven, water can eventually enter and weaken an otherwise excellent board[/28]. It is detailed work, and rushing it can waste everything done earlier. || Presenter: Do you ever watch customers using your boards? || Tom: Whenever I can. A customer may struggle to explain what feels wrong, whereas watching reveals useful clues. [29]Seeing how somebody balances and turns on a board often tells me more than their description in the workshop[/29]. Afterwards we can discuss small adjustments or choose a better design next time. || Presenter: What is the best part of making boards by hand? || Tom: It is not producing as many as possible; factories already do that. [30]The satisfaction comes when someone returns after months of use and says a board has helped them enjoy the sea more confidently[/30]. Then the hours of measuring, sanding and cleaning dust from the workshop seem worthwhile.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What led Tom to start making surfboards?

options.A: Learning how design affected movement in the sea.
options.B: Wanting a cheaper way to become a surfer.
options.C: Seeing boards being produced in a factory.

questions.explanation: Repairing a crack led him to discover how board changes affect movement.

questions.question: Why does Tom display his first board?

options.A: It is popular with customers who are beginners.
options.B: It illustrates the value of learning from errors.
options.C: It was made from an unusual material.

questions.explanation: He keeps it as a reminder that skill develops through examining mistakes.

questions.question: How does Tom decide what board to recommend?

options.A: He considers the customer's experience and surfing location.
options.B: He copies boards used by successful professionals.
options.C: He asks customers to test several finished boards.

questions.explanation: He asks where and how confidently the customer surfs before choosing a shape.

questions.question: What is Tom's approach to alternative materials?

options.A: He only uses materials that can be completely recycled.
options.B: He avoids them because they are less attractive.
options.C: He uses recycled material after checking durability.

questions.explanation: He tests that partly recycled foam is strong enough for safe use.

questions.question: What can happen if the outer coating is badly applied?

options.A: A board can become too heavy to carry.
options.B: Water may enter and damage the board.
options.C: The shape may no longer suit its owner.

questions.explanation: Uneven protective coating can let water in and weaken the board.

questions.question: Why does Tom watch customers surfing?

options.A: It gives him more useful information than words alone.
options.B: It enables him to make repairs on the beach.
options.C: It helps him promote boards through videos.

questions.explanation: Watching balance and turns provides clues customers may not describe.

questions.question: What gives Tom most satisfaction?

options.A: Selling a board to a professional surfer.
options.B: Producing more boards than larger businesses.
options.C: Hearing that his board improved someone's experience.

questions.explanation: He is happiest when a returning customer says the board built confidence and enjoyment.
